options.A: Template (tailored for your situation): - Header: Client, Matter ID, Assigned by [Your name], Date, Reason for delegation (note: after a 48-hour silent residency you experienced severe, disorienting headaches and delegated precedent-heavy work to protect workflow). - Scope & objective: Precisely list legal questions to answer (e.g., element-by-element analysis of X claim; whether precedent in Y jurisdiction controls; conflicts between appellate panels). - Key jurisdictions (prioritized): Primary jurisdiction (state trial/appellate/court of last resort), controlling federal circuits, and any foreign/common-law jurisdictions expressly relied upon. - Preferred sources (ranked): official reporters & published opinions, state statutes and annotated codes, leading treatises and Restatements, Shepardizing/KeyCite history, recent law-review surveys, firm precedents/memos. - Deliverables & format: (1) Annotated case list with one-line holdings and relevance tags; (2) Short annotated excerpts (no more than 3–4 sentences each) of dispositive passages; (3) One-paragraph executive summary that states the bottom-line rule, how it applies to the facts, and recommended next steps; (4) Attach PDF copies or links to primary authorities. - Instructions to associate: Flag any opinions with unusually dense or technical holdings and provide a 2–3 sentence plain-English distillation; if you encounter neurological-style overload points (e.g., multi-page chained dicta), note them and propose whether deeper review is required. - Time & check-ins: Estimate hours, require an initial 30-minute check-in after preliminary search, and a final review meeting to go over the one-paragraph summary only (delegator reads summary and key flags rather than full cases). - Quality controls: Require citation verification, short methodology note (databases searched, search terms, date ranges), and a short risk/uncertainty note identifying contested or unsettled areas. - Delegation checklist (to attach): Confirm scope, confirm jurisdictions and sources, confirm deadline & estimated hours, confirm check-in times, confirm final deliverable form. options.A: Leave the issue alone for now and make a major change later without checking the current constraints.

Rationale: your earlier decision to reject a standard backtest because a prior numerical test underfunded a seasonal cooperative shows the greatest risk is that aggregate historical averages miss seasonal and community-specific needs; interviews surface those nuances. One-page template (stakeholder-interview structure): - Title & purpose (1 sentence): capture seasonal needs and allocation priorities for community-aligned grants - Context (2 lines): note constraints (endowment size, payout policy, timeline) - Stakeholder list (rows): name/role, primary months of activity, typical revenue sources, space/use notes - Interview prompts (bullet list): 1) Describe your annual cash-flow cycle and peak/lean months; 2) Which months are critical for operations?; 3) What are typical one-off expenses (seasonal supplies, residency costs)?; 4) What program outcomes does additional funding in lean months produce?; 5) What short-term reserve or bridge funding would avert studio closures?; 6) Any non-monetary supports needed?; 7) How would you measure success after allocation? - Synthesis fields (fill-in): common seasonal pain points, high-priority months, recommended allocation buckets (operating bridge, program seed, capital reserve) with suggested percentage ranges, quick decision rules (e.g., hold X% in rolling 3-month reserve if >Y seasonal variance) - Next steps / sign-off: who consolidates interviews, deadline. 90-second facilitator script: "Thank you all — we’re using short stakeholder interviews to make allocations that reflect the cooperative’s seasonal realities. I’ll ask each organization three focused questions about their annual cash flow, the months when funding is most critical, and what a short-term grant would accomplish. Please be specific about timing and one-off costs. We’ll capture themes and translate them into allocation buckets with simple decision rules so we avoid repeating past misallocations. Each interview will take 15 minutes; I’ll synthesize into recommendations within one week. Any questions before we begin?" This answer uses the prior incident (the underfunded seasonal cooperative and your choice to avoid a backtest) to justify the interview approach and to tailor prompts to seasonal cash-flow risks.
options.D: Either structure can work; choose by goal. Sample one-page interview template (concise): Title/purpose; context (size, payout rule); stakeholder table; 5 interview prompts about timing, costs, outcomes, fallback plans, metrics; synthesis: top 3 issues, recommended buckets, simple decision rules; next steps. Short facilitator script: explain purpose, set time per interview, ask for specific months/amounts, promise synthesis and timeline. If you prefer scenario narratives instead, the one-page template would list 3–4 scenarios (e.g., high-tourist season, low-ticket winter, emergency vacancy), expected impacts, allocation responses for each, and monitoring triggers. Pick interviews if you want granular community context; pick narratives if you need a compact board discussion.
